coredata.dc:description: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) synthesized from chain functional monomers are restricted by spatial extension and exhibit relatively poor affinity and selectivity; this results in unsatisfactory applications in complex media. In this study, we prepared unique spherical carbonized polymer dots (CPDs-OH) via the incomplete carbonization of 1-allyl-3-vinylimidazolium bromide and ethylene glycol, and used it as a functional monomer to prepare a newly imprinted polymer (CPDs-OH@MIP) in aqueous media. As a result, the CPDs-OH@MIP exhibited effective recognition of oxytetracycline with an impressive imprinting factor of 6.17, surpassing MIPs prepared with chain functional monomers (1–3). Furthermore, CPDs-OH@MIP exhibited excellent adsorption for oxytetracycline (278.52 mg g−1) and achieved equilibrium in 30 min, with stronger resistance to coexisting cations, anions, and humic acid. Compared to other MIPs and adsorbents, the recognition performance of CPDs-OH@MIP improved 2–4 times; this polymer could remove >92.1% of oxytetracycline in real water samples with at least 10 cycle times. CPDs-OH@MIP prepared using the special spherical monomer forms a denser structure with fewer nonimprinted regions and precisely imprinted sites, remarkably improving the affinity and selectivity of MIPs combined via hydrogen bonds and electrostatic and π-π interactions. Our proposed strategy provides an effective basis for breakthroughs in the practical application of MIPs.

head.ce:title: A novel carbonized polymer dots-based molecularly imprinted polymer with superior affinity and selectivity for oxytetracycline removal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel MIP was synthesized by using carbonized polymer dots CPDs-OH as functional monomer. The employed special spherical monomer generated more precisely imprinted sites and fewer nonimprinted regions, remarkably improving the affinity and selectivity of MIPs for OTC in complicated matrix compared to traditional chain functional monomers.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) synthesized from chain functional monomers are restricted by spatial extension and exhibit relatively poor affinity and selectivity; this results in unsatisfactory applications in complex media. In this study, we prepared unique spherical carbonized polymer dots (CPDs-OH) via the incomplete carbonization of 1-allyl-3-vinylimidazolium bromide and ethylene glycol, and used it as a functional monomer to prepare a newly imprinted polymer (CPDs-OH@MIP) in aqueous media. As a result, the CPDs-OH@MIP exhibited effective recognition of oxytetracycline with an impressive imprinting factor of 6.17, surpassing MIPs prepared with chain functional monomers (1–3). Furthermore, CPDs-OH@MIP exhibited excellent adsorption for oxytetracycline (278.52 mg g−1) and achieved equilibrium in 30 min, with stronger resistance to coexisting cations, anions, and humic acid. Compared to other MIPs and adsorbents, the recognition performance of CPDs-OH@MIP improved 2–4 times; this polymer could remove >92.1% of oxytetracycline in real water samples with at least 10 cycle times. CPDs-OH@MIP prepared using the special spherical monomer forms a denser structure with fewer nonimprinted regions and precisely imprinted sites, remarkably improving the affinity and selectivity of MIPs combined via hydrogen bonds and electrostatic and π-π interactions. Our proposed strategy provides an effective basis for breakthroughs in the practical application of MIPs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with template-directed selectivity have been prepared based on the principle of antigen–antibody recognition. One of the most attentions is focused on the development and application of MIPs, aimed at improving their selectivity and practical in different fields [1,2]. MIPs exhibit specific recognition abilities owing to their template-created cavities that are complementary to the shape of the template, and also possess functional groups that can interact with the template [3–5]. However, most reported MIPs exhibit relatively low imprinting factors and poor anti-interference properties, resulting in insufficient specific recognition abilities in complex media. To overcome these issues, many imprinting strategies have been proposed to improve the performance of MIPs and enhance the practical applicability of imprinted materials in complex systems. These strategies aim to improve MIPs performance by introducing multiple monomer species to increase the number of binding sites and enhance interactions with the template or by modifications after imprinting to improve the accuracy of the binding sites and decrease the presence of nonbinding sites. Some of the reported methods include epitope imprinting [6], post-imprinting modification [7,8], cladding [9–11], solid-phase synthesis [12,13], and dual/multifunctional monomer imprinting [14]. Although these methods have exhibited some improvements, the MIPs are still synthesized using traditional chain functional monomers such as methacrylic acid, vinyl pyridine, acrylamide, and alkoxysilane. Owing to the inherent structural features of these chain functional monomers, restricted conformational flexibility and steric hindrance impede the generation of highly specific binding sites within the imprinted layer. As a result, the binding affinity and selectivity of the obtained MIPs toward the target molecules are compromised, with low imprinting factors (1–3) and poor anti-interference capabilities [15–17]; this, in turn, results in poor adsorption selectivity in complex media, impeding the practical applications of these MIPs.

Functional monomers are vital for preparing MIPs because they form complexes with templates to generate recognition sites in the polymers [18]. However, the structures of these conventional monomers are limited to chains; furthermore, the restricted spatial orientation of the recognition unit (functional groups) and polymerizable unit (unsaturated double bond) in the polymerization system impede the effective binding of the functional monomers to the template molecules. Therefore, the spatial arrangement of the template and functional monomers is nonhomogeneous; this leads to the formation of inaccurately imprinted sites during the pre-polymerization phase [19]. After polymerization, the functional monomers failing to form complexes either undergo self-polymerization or remain as disordered arrangements on the polymer surface, resulting in the generation of several nonimprinted regions. These nonimprinted regions are also present in the action groups of the polymer, resulting in nonspecific adsorption. Therefore, the formation of incorrect imprinted sites and several nonimprinted regions inevitably results in significant nonspecific adsorption, thereby limiting the recognition ability of the prepared MIPs [9–11,20]. Therefore, designing and synthesizing functional monomers with novel structures to obtain MIPs with superior recognition abilities are urgently warranted to address these limitations.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) are a class of carbon nanomaterials characterized by spherical carbon cores. The nanohybrid structure of the polymer shell not only retains the advantages of traditional carbon dots but also exhibits the unique properties of polymers [21–23], including the ability to design functional groups, facile doping of heteroatoms, and easy formation of functionalized composites with other materials. Furthermore, CPDs possess an unparalleled specific surface area, and their surfaces are rich in functional groups. This, combined with their extraordinary hydrophilicity, allows CPDs to interact more effectively with contaminant molecules, thereby exhibiting exceptional adsorption ability [24–29]. Therefore, the functionalized CPDs can be achieved by introducing various recognition units that can interact with the target and polymerized units to participate in the polymerization reaction, which can be used as new functional monomers for the synthesis of MIPs. Because the functionalized surface of spherical CPDs possesses a higher density of recognition groups, it can interact with groups in the template molecule in any direction and angle; this facilitates accurate alignment with the template molecules to form more precise imprinting sites. In addition, the unique zero-dimensional structure of CPDs can help generate a more compact and orderly spatial arrangement of the formed polymer, thereby decreasing the formation of nonimprinted regions. Subsequently, MIPs with more precisely imprinted sites and fewer nonimprinted regions can significantly improve the specific recognition abilities of these MIPs in complex systems.

Oxytetracycline (OTC) is a widely used antibiotic for treating livestock infections because of its potent antimicrobial properties, low cost, and minimal adverse effects [30–33]. However, its inherent chemical stability and prolonged half-life, attributed to the polyaromatic ring structure of OTC, have led to the alarming issue of antibiotic pollution [34,35]. This has significant implications for human health and ecosystem sustainability [36]. The adsorption and removal of antibiotics using MIPs have recently received considerable attention [15,37,38]. However, MIPs prepared using conventional chain functional monomers exhibit poor selectivity and interference resistance in complex and heterogeneous media. This results in the lower adsorption capacity and imprinting factor of the polymer toward the target, thereby limiting their application as effective adsorbents for real environmental samples.

In the present study, we proposed a special spheroidal CPDs functional monomer to improve the recognition capabilities of MIPs. The ionic liquid 1-allyl-3-vinylimidazolium bromide with vinyl double bonds was used as the polymer unit precursor and ethylene glycol with hydroxyl groups was used as the functional group precursor. Hydroxyl-functionalized CPDs (CPDs-OH) were prepared by the cross-linking and incomplete carbonization of the precursor; the prepared polymer dots were used as a unique functional monomer to fabricate an OTC-imprinted polymer (CPDs-OH@MIP) on the surface of MIL-53(Fe) in aqueous media. The structure and morphology of CPDs-OH@MIP were characterized, and the adsorption properties and possible adsorption mechanisms were investigated. Finally, the affinity and selectivity of CPDs-OH@MIP for OTC were investigated in seven complex real water samples.

1-allyl-3-vinylimidazolium bromide (99%) was procured from Chengjie Chemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Oxytetracycline (95%), Tetracycline (96%), Chlorotetracycline (95%), Doxycycline (97%), and Ciprofloxacin (98%), acrylic acid (99%), methacrylic acid (99%), acrylamide (99%), and 2-acrylamido-2-methylpropanesulfonic acid (98%) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ofloxacin (98%), Levofloxacin (98%), Pefloxacin (98%), glacial acetic acid (99.9%), Bisphenol A (99%), Bisphenol B (98%), and Diclofenac Sodium (99%) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). N, N'-methylenebisacrylamide (99%) was bought from J&K Chem. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Azodiisobutyronitrile (99%) is produced by Beijing Chemical Industry Co. (Beijing, China). Ferrous chloride (FeCl2⋅6H2O, 99.0%), and N, N-dimethylformamide (99.5%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Chromatographic grade methanol was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, USA), and ultrapure water was purchased from Wahaha Co. Ltd. (Hangzhou, China).

The morphologies of samples were observed with a scanning electron microscopy (JSM-5610LV, JEOL, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy images were obtained with an electron microscope (Jem-2100F, Jeol, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were taken in a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, Palo Alto, USA). UV–vis spectra (T6, Pgeneral, Beijing, China). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Thermo Nicolet Co., USA) at 4000–400 cm−1 and X-ray powder diffraction (Brukeraxs Co., Germany) was used to determine the structure of samples. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms were measured at 77.3 K by a physisorption analyzer (ASAP 2020, Micromeritics, USA). The thermal stability of the polymers was evaluated by a STA449C thermal analyzer (Netzsch, Germany). The elements and structures of samples by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (ESCALAB250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA). 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) was recorded on a AVANCE NEO spectrometer (400 MHz, Bruker, Germany). The values of pH at the point of zero charge (pHzpc) of samples were analyzed using Zeta Potential Analyzer (zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK).

First, CPDs-OH were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 1-allyl-3-vinyl imidazolium bromide (1.9360 g, 0.009 mol) and ethylene glycol (0.3103 g, 0.005 mol) were added to a polytetrafluoroethylene reaction kettle containing 10 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 10 min and then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 220°C for 12 h. After cooling to 25°C, the resultant solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane; the unreacted precursors were removed via dialysis (1000 Da dialysis bag for approximately 48 h). Finally, a CPDs-OH solution was prepared and used for subsequent experiments. Fig. 1 a illustrates the schematic diagram for preparing CPDs-OH.

Next, the CPDs-OH@MIP was prepared as follows. Briefly, in the first step, 0.1240 g (0.25 mmol) of OTC (template molecule), 600 μL of CPDs-OH (functional monomer), and 0.15 g of MIL-53(Fe) (carrier) were dispersed into 50 mL of ultrapure water and pre-polymerized at 30°C for 4 h in a water bath oscillator. Thereafter, 0.7708 g (5.00 mmol) of N, N’-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA, crosslinker) and 10 mg of azobisisobutyronitrile (initiator) were dissolved in the aforementioned solution; this solution was deoxygenated for 10 min using nitrogen and then placed in a water bath oscillator for 24 h at 60°C by shaking at the speed of 300 rpm to complete the polymerization process. In the third step, the template molecule was removed via Soxhlet extraction with methanol: acetic acid (9:1, v/v). Finally, CPDs-OH@MIP was obtained by vacuum drying at 60°C for 24 h. The CPDs-OH@NIP was prepared using a similar procedure, without the addition of OTC. Fig. 2 a illustrated the schematic diagram for preparing CPDs-OH@MIP.

Four commonly used traditional chain functional monomers, acrylic acid (AA), methacrylic acid (MAA), acrylamide (AM), and 2-acrylamido-2-methylpropanesulfonic acid (AMPS) were separately used as a control to synthesis the corresponding OTC-imprinted material (AA@MIP, MAA@MIP, AM@MIP, and AMPS@MIP), with all other conditions kept constant (see Supplementary Materials).

Eleven contaminants with different structures (Fig. S1), namely, tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC), and doxycycline (DC), which have structures similar to OTC, ciprofloxacin (CIP), ofloxacin (OFLX), levofloxacin (LEV), pefloxacin (PEF), bisphenol A (BPA), bisphenol B (BPB), and diclofenac sodium (DS), whose structures are relatively different to OTC, were chosen as interferences to determine the adsorption selectivity of the as-prepared CPDs-OH@MIP and AA@MIP via single and competitive selectivity adsorption experiments.

Briefly, 5 mg of CPDs-OH@MIP/NIP and AA@MIP/NIP were individually submerged in 30 mg L−1 (10 mL) aqueous solutions of OTC, CIP, OFLX, LEV, PEF, DS, or BPA. A multisolute adsorption experiment was performed to determine the competitive adsorption of the polymers. Five milligrams of the imprinted material were added to a mixture containing 30 mg L−1 DC, CTC, TC, OTC, OFLX, BPA, and BPB. After shaking for 2 h, the concentration of each substrate was determined using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (see Supplementary Materials). The corresponding adsorption capacity Qe was calculated using Eq. (1) [39].

Adsorption kinetic studies were performed to investigate the effect of adsorption time on the adsorption capacity of CPDs-OH@MIP/NIP and AA@MIP/NIP. Briefly, 5 mg of the imprinted polymers were dispersed in the OTC solution (30 mg L−1, 10 mL), followed by shaking for predetermined intervals (1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 55, 70, 90, 110, 135, 160, and 200 min). The concentration of OTC (Ct , mg L−1) in the supernatant was determined using HPLC.

The adsorption performances of CPDs-OH@MIP/NIP and AA@MIP/NIP were determined via isotherm adsorption experiments. Briefly, 5 mg of the imprinted materials were dispersed in the OTC solution (10 mL). The initial concentrations (Ci , mg L−1) ranged from 0.5 to 450 mg L−1 at different temperatures (25, 35, and 45°C). After shaking and achieving adsorption equilibrium, the concentration of OTC in each solution was determined. The adsorption capacity (Qe , mg g−1) of CPD-OH@MIP and AA@MIP for OTC was calculated using Eq. (1).

Seven real water samples, namely, deionized water, rainwater, tap water, river water, lake water, sewage influent, and sewage effluent, were used to practically evaluate the selective removal efficiency of CPDs-OH@MIP and AA@MIP for OTC. First, each sample was spiked with 1, 2, and 5 mg L−1 OTC. Then, all water samples (100 mL) were individually filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane before use. Next, 5 mg of CPDs-OH@MIP or AA@MIP was separately dispersed in 10 mL of the above-pretreated samples. The mixtures were shaken for 1 h at 25°C. Subsequently, each supernatant was analyzed using HPLC. The removal efficiency of the OTC was calculated using Eq. (2) [40]. The concentration of OTC in each unspiked blank sample was also analyzed.

To determine the regeneration ability of the developed CPDs-OH@MIP, 2 mg L−1 of the OTC aqueous solution was passed through a self-made SPE column (see Supplementary Material), followed by washing with a mixed solution of methanol/acetic acid (100/1, v/v). The concentration of OTC in the effluent was determined using HPLC. Subsequently, the adsorption–desorption process was conducted for 10 cycles to evaluate the adsorption performance of the as-prepared CPDs-OH@MIP and AA@MIP toward OTC.
Refer to Supplementary Material for the relevant information.

Fig. 1b illustrates the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) results. TEM revealed that CPDs-OH comprise monodispersed nanodots with a particle diameter of 2.04 ± 0.52 nm (Fig. 1c). From the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images in Fig. 1c, it can be seen the CPDs-OH exhibits a lattice-fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the lattice spacing of the (100) facet of graphitic carbon and belongs to the structure of carbon dots. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CPDs-OH is illustrated in Fig. 1d. CPDs-OH exhibited a broad peak at 25°, which is a characteristic of amorphous carbon and is caused by the deformation of sp3 carbon defects in the graphite structure [22]. These findings indicated the successful synthesis and spherical structure of CPDs-OH. From the UV–Vis absorption spectrum, it can be seen that there exist an obviously absorbance peak at 241 nm, which was attributed to π-π* transition of the C=C and was a sign of the properties of the molecular chromophore (color-generating), indicating the optical properties of CPDs-OH. Additionally, the photoluminescence spectra of CPDs-OH showed that the synthesized CPDs-OH exhibited a conspicuous excitation-dependent multicolor emission feature. This result veified that the synthesized material is carbon dots. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1e) revealed the presence of absorption bands at 1410 cm−1, 1655 cm−1, 2923 cm−1, and 3449 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching modes of C–N, C=C, C–H, and –OH, respectively [41–43]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) peaks of CPDs-OH corresponding to Br3s, C1s, N1s, and O1s were clearly visible at 255.0, 284.5, 398.7, and 531.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 1f). In addition, the high-resolution C1s spectra of CPDs-OH (Fig. 1g) revealed the presence of C=C (284.7 eV), C–N (285.5 eV), C=O (287.0 eV), and C–O (286.2 eV) bonds; and the contents of C=C, C–O, and C=O bonds in CPDs-OH were 27.40%, 20.03%, and 6.87%, respectively. These findings confirm the increase in effective sp2 domains in CPDs-OH. In Fig. 1h, two peaks at 399.1 and 400.5 eV were ascribed to pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively, and the peaks at 531.2 and 532.7 eV In Fig. 1i were C–O and C=O bonds, respectively. XPS analysis (Fig. 1f, g, h, and i) further confirmed the presence of recognition units (functional groups, –OH) and polymerizable units (unsaturated double bonds, C=C) in CPDs-OH; these findings suggest that CPDs-OH can be used as an effective functional monomer. These functional groups facilitate relevant interactions with –OH, –NH2, C=O, and benzene rings on the target template OTC, including hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and π-π interactions, in multiple directions. Furthermore, the C=C bond in CPDs-OH can participate in the polymerization reaction, avoiding CPDs-OH loss during the elution of the imprinting template.

Fig. 2b, c, and d illustrate the surface morphologies of MIL-53(Fe), CPDs-OH@MIP, and CPDs-OH@NIP, respectively, as revealed by scanning electron microscopy. MIL-53(Fe) possessed rod-like crystals and smaller bulky and irregular fragments with smooth surfaces; this finding is consistent with that of previous studies [44,45]. In contrast, the surface morphology and size of CPDs-OH@MIP (Fig. 2c) and CPDs-OH@NIP (Fig. 2d) were significantly altered, indicating that zero-dimensional CPDs-OH was successfully polymerized as a functional monomer on the surface of MIL-53(Fe). High-magnification TEM analysis of MIL-53(Fe) and CPDs-OH@MIP revealed the presence of well-distributed CPDs-OH in the imprinted layers (Fig. 2e, f). Further magnification revealed that CPDs-OH did not exist as individual dots but rather were wrapped in the polymer layer (Fig. 2g). HRTEM (Fig. 2h) was utilized to further analyze whether CPDs-OH has been successfully introduced into the imprinted layers. As can be seen, the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon was detected, illustrating the existence of CPDs-OH [28], this statement suggests the active participation of CPDs-OH in the polymerization process, with effective dispersion within the polymer. This statement suggests the active participation of CPDs-OH in the polymerization process, with effective dispersion within the polymer.

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2i, curve i) CPDs-OH revealed the stretching vibrations of C–OH/N–H at 3430 cm−1 [46] and C–H at 2923 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 [18], vibrational absorption band of C=C at 1655 cm−1, and bending vibrations of N–H at 1570 cm−1 [47]; this finding indicates that the prepared CPDs-OH contain abundant functional and polymeric groups. Both CPDs-OH@MIP (curve iii) and CPDs-OH@NIP (curve iv) contained the characteristic peaks at approximately 3430 and 2976 cm−1, with distinctly different MIL-53(Fe) (curves ii), which were considered the vibration absorption of the C–OH and C–H bonds from CPDs-OH (curves i); this demonstrates successful polymerization on the surface of MIL-53(Fe). The vibrational absorption peaks of CPDs-OH@MIP (curves iii) and CPDs-OH@NIP (curves iv) were similar, indicating that OTC was completely removed.

In Fig. 2j, the XPS spectral signals at 285.0, 532.0, and 713.0 eV were assigned to C1s, O1s, and Fe2p, respectively [48]. The appearance of a new signal at 400.1 eV for CPDs-OH@MIP and CPDs-OH@NIP represented N1s, which may correspond to the N from CPDs-OH or N from the crosslinker. These results indicate the successful polymerization of the functional monomer and crosslinker. The XRD patterns of MIL-53(Fe), CPDs-OH@MIP, and CPDs-OH@NIP (Fig. S3) exhibited the typical diffraction peaks of MIL-53(Fe) at 2θ = 9.4°, 12.8°, 17.8°, 25.6°, and 27.5° [49]. After polymerization, the intensity of the characteristic peak of MIL-53(Fe) dramatically decreased for CPDs-OH@MIP and CPDs-OH@NIP.

The thermal stabilities of MIL-53(Fe), CPDs-OH@MIP, and CPDs-OH@NIP were evaluated under a nitrogen atmosphere. Fig. 2k illustrates that MIL-53(Fe) demonstrated high stability at a temperature of < 130°C; the mass loss between 130°C and 650°C was approximately 60.01%, which was mostly attributable to the breakdown of solvents and small molecules. Furthermore, CPDs-OH@MIP and CPDs-OH@NIP exhibited excellent stability at temperatures of < 130°C; the mass loss between 130°C and 650°C was similar to that of the carrier. This indicates that the addition of CPDs-OH did not affect the thermal stability of CPDs-OH@MIP, which is beneficial for practical use.

To investigate the selectivity of the imprinted materials, four conventional chain functional monomers, namely, AA, MAA, AM, AMPS, and the developed spheroidal CPDs-OH functional monomer were chosen to prepare OTC-imprinted polymers under the same conditions (Table S1). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the adsorption capacity and imprinting factor of the four MIPs prepared using the chain functional monomers were 12.73–51.14 mg g−1 and 0.75–2.26, respectively; these values were relatively lower than those of spheroidal CPDs-OH (58.92 mg g−1 and 6.06, respectively). This indicates that the spheroidal functional monomer CPDs-OH effectively improves the performance of MIPs. Because the MIP prepared using AA had better adsorption performance, the imprinted materials prepared using the spherical and chain monomers were optimized (Table S2, S3) and compared with CPDs-OH@MIP and AA@MIP in the subsequent experiments.

The single selectivity experiment (Fig. 3b) revealed that CPDs-OH@MIP exhibited a higher adsorption capacity toward OTC (58.92 mg g−1); however, the adsorption capacity for other nontarget pollutants such as CIP, OFLX, LEV, PEF, DS, and BPA was significantly lower (7.06–19.86 mg g−1). Furthermore, the adsorption capacity of CPDs-OH@NIP toward all the tested substrates was very low (2.30–9.86 mg g−1). In contrast, as shown in Fig. 3c, the adsorption capacity of AA@MIP for OTC was 51.14 mg g−1, whereas that for CIP, OFLX, LEV, PEF, DS, and BPA was 11.69–37.79 mg g−1. On the other hand, the adsorption capacity of AA@NIP for all pollutants was approximately 9.37–47.18 mg g−1. Taken together, these findings indicate that compared with AA as a conventional chain functional monomer, the imprinting sites in CPDs-OH@MIP were highly matched with the template molecule.

The competitive selectivity experiment (Fig. 3d) revealed the excellent binding capacity of CPDs-OH@MIP for OTC and its structural analogs DC, TCT, and TC (40.58–50.65 mg g−1) in mixed solution; however, its binding capacity for the reference pollutants OFLX, BPA, and BPB was < 8.91 mg g−1. Furthermore, CPDs-OH@NIP exhibited very poor binding capacities for all eight substrates (0.09–2.90 mg g−1). Fig. 3e demonstrates that the binding capacities of AA@MIP and AA@NIP for the seven substrates exhibited no obvious differences, ranging from 10.50 to 27.72 mg g−1. It can be seen that the adsorption capacity and imprinting factor of CPDs-OH@MIP (40.58 mg g−1, 16.83) were dramatically increased by 3.86 and 4.76 times, respectively, compared with AA@MIP (10.5 mg g−1, 3.53). Besides, the selectivity parameters (Table S4) revealed that the Kd values of CPDs-OH@MIP for OTC, DC, TCT, and TC (2.089–5.417 L g−1) were significantly higher than those for the reference compounds OFLX (0.174 L g−1), BPA (0.053 L g−1), and BPB (0.034 L g−1). While as the Kd values of AA@MIP for OTC and structurally similar compounds were only 0.212–0.859 L g−1, comparable to those of the reference compounds OFLX (0.336 L g−1), BPA (0.250 L g−1), and BPB (0.363 L g−1). Furthermore, heightened K and K' values correspond to increased material selectivity. In this investigation, CPDs-OH@MIP exhibited notably elevated K values (11.998, 39.702, 60.598) and K' values (13.900, 20.235, 28.263) for reference compounds OFLX, BPA, and BPB, surpassing those of AA@MIP (0.630, 0.848, 0.583) and K' (1.590, 2.726, 1.576). This discrepancy underscores the superior recognition capability of CPDs-OH@MIP.

Collectively, these results indicate that compared with traditional chain functional monomers, the unique spherical structure and zero-dimensional size of CPDs-OH provide higher accuracy in matching the imprinted sites with the template molecules. The generated binding sites were not only rich but also more accurate, promoting a more uniform and dense spatial arrangement of the binding sites in the obtained MIPs, significantly decreasing the generation of nonimprinted regions. Therefore, the as-prepared CPDs-OH@MIP can effectively recognize OTC in complex matrices, considerably enhancing the practical application of these imprinted materials in complex samples.

The kinetic curves in Fig. 3g revealed that the adsorption of OTC by CPDs-OH@MIP first rapidly increased, slowed down over time, and eventually adsorption capacity reached (59.60 mg g−1) and equilibrium at 30 min; this indicates that the imprinting sites in CPDs-OH@MIP are abundant and effective and can play a vital role in a short period. In AA@MIP from the chain functional monomer, the inaccurate imprinting sites exhibited higher resistance when identifying targets, and the inaccessible imprinting sites not only resulted in a lower adsorption capacity (52.14 mg g−1) but also required a longer equilibrium time (∼100 min). On the other hand, CPDs-OH@NIP prepared using a spherical monomer had a dense structure and fewer nonimprinted cavities; therefore, the adsorption was nonselective and random. Furthermore, its adsorption capacity was very low (7.72 mg g−1), and adsorption equilibrium time (30 min) was significantly higher than that of AA@NIP (∼75 min). In addition, the effect of temperature showed that the rate of adsorption of OTC on CPDs-OH@MIP increased as the test temperature was increased from 25°C to 45°C, and the time to reach adsorption equilibrium was shorter than that of AA@MIP (Fig. S4). These results suggest that the strategy of using a spherical monomer provides breakthroughs for effectively exploring and promoting the practical performance of MIPs.

To model the adsorption kinetics process, we used pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic [50] and elovich models [51]. Table 1 lists the nonlinear fitting relevant parameters. The pseudo-second-order kinetic model had higher adjusted correlation coefficients (R2 adj, 0.9848–0.9957) and lower standard deviations (1.2319–2.2759); therefore, it is suitable for describing the adsorption behavior of CPDs-OH@MIP and AA@MIP for OTC. The pseudo-second-order kinetic model revealed that the adsorption behavior is more consistent with the chemisorption mechanism [51], that is, chemical bond formation and breaking during adsorption, rather than simple physical adsorption. This indicates that the imprinting sites in CPDs-OH@MIP combine with the target via chemical reactions, improving its capacity and adsorption efficiency.

Fig. 3h illustrates the isothermal adsorption curves of CPDs-OH@MIP/NIP and AA@MIP/NIP. The adsorption capacities of CPDs-OH@MIP and CPDs-OH@NIP for OTC gradually increased with increasing OTC concentration. The maximal adsorption capacity (Qe ) of OTC onto CPDs-OH@MIP was 278.52 mg g−1; however, that of CPD-OH@NIP was only 45.14 mg g−1; this indicates that the dense spatial structure based on the spherical functional monomer effectively inhibited the formation of nonimprinted regions; as a result, the adsorption capacity of CPDs-OH@NIP was remarkably decreased, resulting in a surprising imprinting factor of 6.17, which was significantly higher than that of reported MIPs prepared using traditional chain functional monomers. For AA@MIP and AA@NIP, the Qe was 155.89 and 90.82 mg g−1, respectively. The lower Qe of AA@MIP and higher Qe of AA@NIP resulted in an unsatisfactory imprinting factor of 1.72, suggesting that insufficient effective imprinting sites and a large number of nonimprinted regions in MIPs prepared using chain monomers are not conducive to identifying targets, leading to weak competitiveness in practical applications. Moreover, the effect of temperature revealed that the adsorption of OTC onto CPDs-OH@MIP increased with an increase in the tested temperature from 25°C to 45°C (Fig. S5); this indicates that higher temperatures are advantageous for the adsorption process. The imprinting factor was 3.79–6.17, which was still better than that of the reported MIPs; therefore, CPDs-OH@MIP has stable and excellent affinity and selectivity.

To further discuss the adsorption process, three isotherm models (Langmuir, Freundlich and Temkin adsorption isotherm models) [33,52,53], were used to fit the experimental data. As shown in Table 2 , the adsorption of OTC by CPDs-OH@MIP was more consistent with the Langmuir model, with the highest R2 adj value (0.9322–0.9816); on the other hand, the adsorption of OTC by AA@MIP was more consistent with the Freundlich model, with the highest R2 adj value (0.8889–0.9717). These findings imply that the adsorption process of CPDs-OH@MIP for the target occurred on a fixed number of adsorption sites, whereas that of AA@MIP was multimolecular and heterogeneous [54]. In addition, this indicates that the rich and precise binding sites in CPDs-OH@MIP can be fully exposed and utilized, conducive to identifying target pollutants.

The adsorption behavior of OTC onto CPDs-OH@MIP was determined at 25, 35, and 45°C using ΔG0, ΔH0, and ΔS0, respectively (see Supplementary Materials). The results are displayed in Table S5. In general, the absolute value of ΔG0 for physical adsorption (0–20 kJ mol−1) was smaller than that for chemisorption (80–200 kJ mol−1) [55]. In the present study, all ΔG0 values were negative and decreased as temperature increased, indicating that the adsorption of OTC onto CPDs-OH@MIP was spontaneous and feasible. On the other hand, the absolute ΔG0 values for CPDs-OH@MIP, AA@MIP, and AA@NIP were at 18.98–26.53, 40.17–46.26, and 39.37–44.40 kJ mol−1, respectively. These values are close to the typical values reported in the literature for hydrogen bond formation and electrostatic interactions [56], suggesting that the adsorption of OTC onto these materials involves both physical and chemical adsorption. Typically, the absolute ΔG0 value for CPDs-OH@NIP toward OTC was 11.91–18.22 kJ mol−1; this was primarily owing to physical adsorption, confirming that the spherical functional monomer effectively decreased the presence of ineffective three-dimensional pores in the imprinted materials and that the adsorption of CPDs-OH@NIP is only random surface adsorption. On the other hand, all the positive ΔH0 and ΔS0 values indicated endothermic adsorption and an increase in randomness during the adsorption process; these changes resulted in the release of small water molecules during OTC adsorption onto the imprinted materials [57].

Industrial wastewater and natural water generally contain complex matrices that affect the adsorption of OTC. Therefore, we evaluated the effects of common interferences such as cations, anions, and humic acids in water on OTC removal (see Supplementary Materials). Fig. 4 illustrates that all the tested cations, anions, and humic acids had less effect on CPDs-OH@MIP; however, among the eight cations, only Cu2+ (0.2 and 2 mmol L−1) affected OTC removal, with removal efficiency decreasing from 96.74% to 53.98%. In general, the concentration of Cu2+ in real samples is low 0.02 mmol L−1 [58]. In our study, we carefully evaluated the effect of different concentrations of Cu2+ ions (from 0 to 0.1 mmol L−1) on the OTC removal effect of CPDs-OH@MIP. As depicted in Fig. S6, the outcomes distinctly affirm the unaltered removal efficiency of CPDs-OH@MIP within this concentration range. In contrast, except for humic acid, both cations and anions had more significant effects on the removal of OTC by AA@MIP. Furthermore, the presence of Fe3+, Fe2+, Ca+, and Cu2+ decreased the removal efficiency from 88.34% to 73.48%, 68.04%, 79.29%, and 43.51%, respectively, whereas the presence of CO3 2− and HCO3 − led to a decrease in the removal rates from 88.34% to 53.83% and 68.73%, respectively. Taken together, these findings suggest that the spheroidal functional monomer forms a denser structure and fewer nonimprinted regions in CPDs-OH@MIP, resulting in a stronger resistance to coexisting ions, making it more practical for the removal of pollutants.

To elucidate the adsorption mechanism, the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of CPDs-OH@MIP/NIP and AA@MIP/NIP were investigated; they are presented in Fig. 5 , and the textural parameters are summarized in Table S6. All the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller curves exhibited similar type II isotherms, indicating the presence of a mesoporous structure. The most probable pore size of CPDs-OH@MIP/NIP were determined to be 14.77 nm and 13.70 nm, accompanied by pore volumes of 0.251 cm3 g−1 and 0.129 cm3 g−1, respectively. These values were found to be notably smaller than those observed for AA@MIP/NIP, which exhibited most probable pore size of 17.78 nm and 75.13 nm, along with corresponding pore volumes of 0.341 cm3 g−1 and 0.537 cm3 g−1. In comparison to AA@MIP/NIP, the relatively narrower pore size observed in CPDs-OH@MIP/NIP materials signifies an enhanced compatibility between the imprinted binding sites and the template molecules, leading to a higher level of molecular congruence. This, in turn, results in a more precise formation of imprinted binding sites. Furthermore, the decreased pore volume is indicative of an effective suppression and reduction of non-imprinted regions. This indicates that the self-assembly of the spherical functional monomer CPDs-OH with a template molecule is highly autonomous during the polymerization process and can inhibit the formation of ineffective pores and nonimprinted regions. Moreover, the efficient utilization of surface groups and ordered spherical array arrangement of CPDs-OH are conducive to the formation of more precise and effective imprinting sites in CPDs-OH@MIP [59]; therefore, both affinity and selectivity were dramatically improved. Table S6 shows that the specific surface area of MIL-53(Fe) was 8.2 m2 g−1 and that the specific surface area of all the imprinted materials was increased, suggesting the formation of an imprinted layer on the surface of the carriers. The specific surface areas of CPDs-OH@MIP and CPDs-OH@NIP were 28.6 and 33.9 m2 g−1, respectively. The slight difference between them illustrates that the improved adsorption of CPDs-OH@MIP is not associated with its surface area but may depend on more precisely matched imprinting sites. In contrast, AA@MIP and AA@NIP had higher specific surface areas of 43.5 and 99.6 m2 g−1, respectively; this was probably owing to the inaccurate match of the chain monomer with the template in the spatial orientation, resulting in self-aggregation and nonimprinted cavities in the MIPs. These ineffective specific surface areas did not improve the adsorption performance of the polymers.

Next, we explored the effects of pH and zeta potential on CPDs-OH@MIP and AA@MIP. As shown in Fig. 6 a, c, CPDs-OH@MIP exhibited excellent adsorption stability and was almost unaffected at pH 4–9. However, the adsorption capacity significantly decreased at pH values less than 4 (20.71%) and more than 9 (23.50%–82.40%), and the trend presented by CPDs-OH@NIP was essentially the same as that of CPDs-OH@MIP. This may be related to the pKa values of OTC (3.2–9.6) [60]. OTC is present in a neutral state as zwitterions with a pH of 3.3–9.6 and primarily exists in the form of cations (<3.3) and anions (>9.6). In the present study, the zeta potential (pHzpc) of CPDs-OH@MIP was 3.51 (AA@MIP, 3.36) (Fig. 6b, d). Within the pH range of 4–9, the dominant electrostatic attraction between OTC and CPDs-OH@MIP facilitated better adsorption, whereas electrostatic repulsion led to a decrease in adsorption capacity beyond that pH range (<4 and >9). For AA@MIP, the effect of pH on adsorption performance was similar to that of CPDs-OH@MIP. Therefore, electrostatic interactions are not the primary driving force or causative factor behind the distinct differences in the adsorption performance of these two materials.

Further, 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectroscopy (Fig. 6e, f) of OTC (0.25 mmol L−1), CPDs-OH (1.5 mmol L−1), OTC (0.25 mmol L−1) + CPDs-OH (1.5 mmol L−1), AA (1.5 mmol L−1), and OTC (0.25 mmol L−1) + AA (1.5 mmol L−1) in DMSO revealed that the hydroxyl and amino protons in OTC underwent a significant shift. In the mixture of OTC + CPDs-OH (Fig. 6e), protons (1) and (2) shifted upfield from 11.639 and 4.483 ppm to 11.629 and 4.359 ppm, respectively, whereas protons (5) and (6) shifted downfield from 9.118 and 5.081 ppm to 9.134 and 5.102 ppm, respectively. These shifts confirmed the presence of hydrogen bonds between OTC and CPDs-OH (–OH and –NH2) [61]. Similarly, the protons in OTC shifted in the OTC + AA mixture. As shown in Fig. 6f, protons (1), (2), (5), and (6) shifted upfield from 11.639, 4.483, 9.118, and 5.081 ppm to 11.638, 4.377, 9.110, and 5.070 ppm, respectively; this indicates the presence of hydrogen bonds between OTC and AA. However, the shift value in OTC + CPDs-OH (0.010–0.124 ppm) was higher than that in OTC + AA (0.001–0.106 ppm), confirming that the hydrogen bonds between CPDs-OH and OTC are stronger than those between AA and OTC. The stronger interaction was owing to the use of the spheroidal functional monomer. Furthermore, the zero-dimensional structure of CPDs-OH could easily and fully form hydrogen bonds with the template molecule, resulting in superior adsorption affinity and selectivity of CPDs-OH@MIP.

Taken together, as shown in Fig. 7 , CPDs-OH@MIP prepared using the spherical functional monomer CPDs-OH forms a denser structure with fewer nonimprinted regions and precisely imprinted sites, exhibiting superior affinity and selectivity toward OTC in complex matrices via a combination of hydrogen bonding electrostatic interactions, and π-π interactions.

In the present study, we compared the performance of CPDs-OH@MIP, AA@MIP, and two commercial solid-phase extraction sorbents: HLB (AMP) and Oasis (Waters). The ability of these columns to selectively extract OTC and its structural analogs from mixed aqueous solutions containing interfering compounds was evaluated using HPLC analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 3f, only the CPDs-OH@MIP column could selectively adsorb OTC and its structural analogs without adsorbing other interfering compounds (curve ii). Although the AA@MIP column exhibited some selectivity for the target compounds, it also adsorbed some interfering compounds (curve iii). In contrast, the commercial columns HLB (AMP) and Oasis (Waters) did not exhibit any selectivity because all compounds were adsorbed (curves iv and v). These results indicate that the MIP prepared using CPDs-OH as a functional monomer exhibits superior selectivity over the traditional AA functional monomer and commercial HLB (AMP) and Oasis (Waters) columns.

Next, the performance of CPDs-OH@MIP was compared with that of other reported MIPs synthesized using traditional chain functional monomers (Table 3 ). The imprinting factor and adsorption capacity of CPDs-OH@MIP were 6.17 and 278.52 mg g−1, respectively; these values were significantly higher than those of reported MIPs (1.55–2.84 and 6.48–64.80 mg g−1, respectively). In addition, the adsorption equilibrium (within 30 min) and recycling (at least 10 times) of CPDs-OH@MIP were superior to those of other MIPs (30–300 min and ≤10 times, respectively). Moreover, excellent CPDs-OH@MIP was prepared in a green aqueous medium, whereas MIPs using traditional functional monomers, including MAA, Styrene, and AMPS, were usually prepared in volatile organic solvents, which are harmful to humans and the environment [2,68,69]. By comparison with currently reported adsorbents (Table S7), we observed that CPDs-OH@MIP has a satisfactory maximum adsorption capacity (278.52 mg g−1), a shorter adsorption equilibrium time (30 min), and better recycling times (>10). Therefore, the spherical functional monomer CPDs-OH used in this study not only effectively improves the performance of MIPs but also enhances their practicality as a green adsorbent in the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in real samples and reusability
ce:section.ce:para: Seven water samples, namely, deionized water, rainwater, tap water, river water, lake water, sewage influent, and sewage effluent, were chosen to further assess the applicability of CPDs-OH@MIP in real environmental samples. The linearity ranges, correlation coefficient, limits of detection, and precision analysis were investigated and summarized in Table S8 (see Supplementary Materials). Table 4 presents the removal efficiency of CPDs-OH@MIP for OTC. The removal efficiencies (R) were >92.1% and relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) was <3.72. In contrast, the removal efficiency (R) of AA@MIP for OTC was just >82.6%, with an RSD (n = 3) of <4.32 (Table S9). This indicates that CPDs-OH@MIP prepared using spherical CPDs as functional monomers exerts a significant advantage in removing pollutants from complex environmental samples.

The stability and regenerative properties of the MIPs are also critical for practical applications [1]. After the adsorption process was completed, the regeneration abilities of CPDs-OH@MIP and AA@MIP were tested. The eluent was optimized; Fig. S8 illustrates the optimal elution conditions. The results are presented in Fig. 3i. After 10 cycles of adsorption–desorption, the removal efficiency of OTC was still >97%. However, the adsorption efficiency of AA@MIP began to decrease after five cycles, and removal efficiencies decreased to approximately 92% only after eight adsorption–desorption regenerations, suggesting that its regeneration ability was inferior to that of CPDs-OH@MIP. Taken together, the novel CPDs-OH@MIP exerts better regeneration and stability, achieving the green and renewable requirements for superior adsorbents in practical applications.

In this study, a unique spherical functional monomer (CPDs-OH) was developed via the incomplete carbonization of 1-allyl-3-vinylimidazolium bromide and ethylene glycol, and used it to prepare a newly imprinted polymer (CPDs-OH@MIP) on the surface of MIL-53(Fe) in aqueous media. The employed special spherical monomer generated more precisely imprinted sites and fewer nonimprinted regions, remarkably improving the affinity and selectivity. Compared with other molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) synthesized using chain functional monomers, CPDs-OH@MIP has superior affinity and selectivity for oxytetracycline (OTC) and its structural analogs in a complicated matrix. The imprinting factor (6.17) of CPDs-OH@MIP for OTC increased by three times, with the maximum adsorption capacity reaching 278.52 mg g−1, and achieving equilibrium in just 30 min. This may be owing to the formation of a uniform and dense spatial arrangement in the MIPs involving the special and zero-dimensional structure of the functional monomer CPDs-OH. As an advanced and environmentally adsorbent, CPDs-OH@MIP can remove >92.1% OTC from real water samples with better regeneration and stability. The proposed strategy for synthesizing MIPs with high selectivity using special spherical functional monomers overcomes the limitations of traditional chain functional monomers and demonstrates considerable advantages in the preparation and application of high-performance imprinted materials, which provides a new idea for the design and synthesis of novelty imprinting materials.
